topic: The trouble with AI benchmarks: what we measure versus what we wish we measured
title: We build great AI benchmarks. We still don't measure what actually matters.
summary: Every popular AI benchmark ends up measuring test-taking skill, not the general reliable capability everyone actually wants from these systems.
listicle: Every benchmark becomes a skill you can cram for. This is not an AI flaw, this is how all tests work, always.
We don't actually want models that score 98% on MMLU. We want models that don't lie to you when you book a doctor.
No benchmark currently measures not making up facts when you are not looking. That is the thing everyone actually needs.
Right now we are very good at measuring how good models are at taking the tests we have made. That is all.
I don't know how to fix this. Nobody I have read that works on this has a complete good answer yet.
thread: This is exactly the 'teaching to the test' problem everyone hated in high school. Nobody thought that produced good outcomes.
Scaling reliably makes benchmark scores go up. It does not reliably make the capability we want go up.
Almost every working researcher knows this gap exists. Very few will say it on the record.
The first good AI evaluation system will not look anything like a multiple choice test.

criticNotes.note: You skip the core problem: we cannot even write a formal definition of 'the thing we care about'. This is not a measurement problem first.
You have not cited any data showing benchmark scores fully decouple from real world performance. There is real correlation here. Don't throw that out.
'Nobody has a good answer' is too broad. People are testing live evaluation and adversarial probing. Be specific about what still fails.
